# NCERT Summary: The s-Block Elements

## Chapter at a Glance

The s-block elements are the elements present in the first two groups (1 and 2) of the periodic table. These elements are characterised by their electronic configuration, which has one or two electrons in the s-orbital of the valence shell. The s-block elements are highly reactive and tend to lose electrons to achieve a noble gas configuration. The chapter discusses the general characteristics, trends, and applications of the s-block elements, including the alkali metals (Group 1) and the alkaline earth metals (Group 2).

## Section-wise Summary

### 13.1 Introduction

The s-block elements are located in the first two groups of the periodic table. They are highly reactive and tend to lose electrons to achieve a noble gas configuration. The elements in Group 1 are known as alkali metals, while those in Group 2 are known as alkaline earth metals.

### 13.2 Electronic Configuration and Atomic Radii

The s-block elements have one or two electrons in the s-orbital of the valence shell. As we move down the group, the atomic radii increase due to the increase in the number of energy levels. The atomic radii of the s-block elements increase as we move down the group, with a greater increase in size for alkali metals than for alkaline earth metals.

### 13.3 Ionisation Energies

The ionisation energies of s-block elements decrease down the group due to the increase in atomic radii. However, the ionisation energies of alkaline earth metals are higher than those of alkali metals due to the presence of two valence electrons in the former.

### 13.4 Electropositive Character

The s-block elements are highly electropositive, meaning they tend to lose electrons to achieve a noble gas configuration. The electropositive character increases down the group, with alkali metals being more electropositive than alkaline earth metals.

### 13.5 Chemical Reactivity

The s-block elements react with water to produce hydrogen gas and an alkaline solution. The reactivity of s-block elements decreases down the group, with alkali metals being more reactive than alkaline earth metals.

### 13.6 Anomalous Properties of the First Element of Each Group

The first element of each group exhibits anomalous properties due to the presence of a half-filled or fully filled subshell. Lithium (Group 1) and Beryllium (Group 2) exhibit anomalous properties, such as high melting and boiling points, due to their small atomic radii and high electronegativities.

### 13.7 Uses of s-Block Elements

The s-block elements have various uses, including the production of sodium and calcium in the form of their chlorides and carbonates, the use of alkaline earth metals in the manufacture of cement and glass, and the application of alkali metals in the production of sodium hydroxide and caustic soda.

## Important NCERT Exercises Insight

* Key concepts tested in NCERT exercises include:
	+ Electronic configuration and atomic radii of s-block elements
	+ Ionisation energies and electropositive character of s-block elements
	+ Chemical reactivity of s-block elements
	+ Anomalous properties of the first element of each group
* What to remember:
	+ S-block elements are highly reactive and tend to lose electrons to achieve a noble gas configuration.
	+ Alkali metals are more reactive than alkaline earth metals.
	+ Ionisation energies of s-block elements decrease down the group.
	+ Electropositive character increases down the group.

## NCERT Exemplar Focus

The NCERT Exemplar provides additional information on the following topics:

* The reactivity of s-block elements with water and acids.
* The preparation and properties of sodium and calcium in the form of their chlorides and carbonates.
* The use of s-block elements in the manufacture of cement and glass.

## Board Exam Relevance

The s-block elements are an important part of the Class 11 Chemistry syllabus. The marks weightage for this chapter is approximately 10-15% of the total marks. The frequently asked topics from this chapter include:

* Electronic configuration and atomic radii of s-block elements.
* Ionisation energies and electropositive character of s-block elements.
* Chemical reactivity of s-block elements.
* Anomalous properties of the first element of each group.

It is essential to understand the key concepts and trends discussed in this chapter to perform well in the board examination.
